articles.headline: Letter from New York colored public school teacher pay discrimination -- 'A Friend of Justice'
articles.author: A Friend of Justice
articles.summary: Anonymous letter exposing pay discrimination at a New York colored public school: the white female teacher of the girls' department is paid $500-600/year plus assistants, while 'Dr. Brown,' the accomplished Black male teacher who has run the boys' department (100 scholars) as de facto principal for nearly a year, receives only a $150 'monitor's pay.' Editorial reply ('Remarks') endorses the charge, praises Dr. Brown's competence, and calls on the Public School Society's Committee on Teachers to correct the injustice; notes the white female teacher reportedly threatened to resign if a Black man were named principal.

articles.headline: Lewis Woodson letter: petition organizing and editor's-salary subscription plan
articles.author: Lewis Woodson
articles.summary: Pittsburgh's Lewis Woodson writes urging systematic petition drives (naming a traveling agent as the best method) and proposes to be 'ONE OF A HUNDRED' Colored American supporters giving $5 each -- or one of fifty giving $10 each -- to fund editor Samuel Cornish's salary. Also includes a real-estate investment advice column continuing from a prior issue, and a circular calling for a general Western New York meeting to organize suffrage petitions, signed 'R.S.'

articles.headline: Camp Meeting notice -- Colored Episcopal Methodists of Pittsburgh
articles.summary: The Colored Episcopal Methodists of Pittsburgh announce a Camp Meeting beginning August 4, 1837 on Jacob Beltzhooven's plantation near the Washington road, six miles from Pittsburgh, with members of the Zanesville, Steubenville, and Uniontown circuits expected to assist. Committee: S. Bryan and G. Coleman; Rev. Thomas Lawrence, preacher in charge.

articles.headline: New Bedford correspondence: Wm. Rotch, brig 'Rising States,' and Rev. Peter Williams's return from the West Indies
articles.author: C.B.R. (Rev. Charles B. Ray)
articles.summary: General Agent Rev. Charles B. Ray's travel letter from New Bedford describes visiting the venerable Quaker abolitionist Wm. Rotch (father of the Bristol County Anti-Slavery Society's president), who subscribed to the paper and recounted his personal experience aiding fugitives; describes the Black-owned, Black-crewed whaling brig 'Rising States,' recently returned short of oil after springing her mainmast, with no mutiny despite rumors, taken as proof colored men can navigate skillfully and maintain discipline. Notes Rev. Peter Williams's recent return from the West Indies.

articles.headline: Pittsburgh Colored Sabbath School report
articles.author: unsigned (Pittsburgh correspondent)
articles.summary: A Pittsburgh correspondent (July 20, 1837) describes years spent teaching at the city's Colored Sabbath School (100 regular scholars, 1 superintendent, 7 male teachers, 9 female teachers), encloses a $5 donation, and reports that 'our inestimable friend' recently gave two well-attended lectures in the city advancing the anti-slavery cause.

articles.headline: Geneva, NY correspondence: planned August 1st celebration
articles.author: J. W. Duffin
articles.summary: Geneva agent J. W. Duffin (July 19, 1837) reports the local people of color have resolved to celebrate the anniversary of British West India Emancipation and raise funds for the Colored American, and requests a copy of the Emancipation Act text and the commemorative ode to read at the celebration.

articles.headline: 'Societies among the People of Color' -- nationwide survey solicitation
articles.summary: The paper solicits statistical returns from every Benevolent, Literary, and Moral society among people of color nationwide (founding date, founders, membership, incorporation status, officers, funds expended, library holdings, etc.) for publication; gives as a sample the officers of an unnamed local society -- Rufus A. Putman, President; James Towle, Secretary; John Pease, Treasurer.

articles.headline: John Randolph of Roanoke's will emancipating his slaves
articles.summary: Reprints the manumission provisions of Virginia statesman John Randolph of Roanoke's will: frees all his slaves, directs his executor to spend up to $8,000 (or more) transporting and settling them in a free state or territory with land grants, and makes specific bequests of livestock, clothing, and annual provisions to named 'faithful servants' Essex and his wife Hetty, and to Queen, Priscilla, John, and other named individuals for their natural lives.

articles.headline: Obituary: Rev. William Levington, Rector of St. James Protestant Episcopal Church, Baltimore
articles.summary: Reports the death (May 17, 1837) of the Rev. William Levington (OCR rendered 'Watton Levescios'), founding rector of St. James Protestant Episcopal Church for people of color in Baltimore. Ordained deacon by Rt. Rev. William White, Bishop of Pennsylvania, in Philadelphia; invited to Baltimore by leading Black congregants (with the approbation of Bishop Kemp of Maryland) to found a school and an Episcopal congregation for people of color; prospects were initially bleak for lack of a building but he eventually secured a room for a day and Sabbath school and worship, and was later ordained priest, serving his Baltimore congregation until his death.

articles.headline: American Moral Reform Society circular -- first annual meeting notice
articles.author: John P. Burr (Chairman), Rev. Morris Brown, Thomas Butler, F. A. Hinton, John B. Roberts, S. H. Gloucester (Secretary)
articles.summary: The Board of Directors of the American Moral Reform Society (Philadelphia) solicits attendance and delegations from auxiliary societies, churches, and Temperance/Peace/Education societies to its first Annual Meeting on the second Monday (14th) of August 1837 in Philadelphia, and requests each locality send detailed statistical returns on its Black population, churches, clergy, schools, literary/benevolent/temperance societies, mechanics, merchants, and real estate holdings for the annual report. Signed at Philadelphia, June 22, 1837.

articles.headline: Real estate fraud warning -- fictitious bonds and mortgages
articles.summary: Continuation of a real-estate investment advice series warns of a wave of swindling via inflated 'paper stock' -- speculative suburban lots mapped and sold at fictitious valuations with fraudulent bonds and mortgages recorded far above true worth; cites a specific case of a Black citizen's $4,000 suburban purchase against which $70,000 in fictitious mortgages had been recorded, and another instance of a $1,200 mortgage offered against property worth only $80-100.

articles.headline: William Yates, Esq.: 'Slavery and Colored People in Delaware'
articles.author: William Yates, Esq.
articles.summary: Philadelphia attorney William Yates reports on a personal fact-finding visit to Delaware (Wilmington, Smyrna, Camden, Dover), giving 1830-census figures (15,885 free colored, 3,292 enslaved, dropping toward 2,700), and a detailed account of Delaware's discriminatory statutes: bans on free Black firearms ownership without annual license, restrictions on religious assembly after 10pm, exclusion of Black testimony against whites, disenfranchisement, ban on interracial marriage, discriminatory school-fund exclusion, near-total absence of schools for Black children outside Wilmington (funded partly by the late Samuel Fisher of Philadelphia), harsh kidnapping penalties undercut by weak enforcement, and mixed local opinion on whether the cause of emancipation was advancing or retrograding.

articles.headline: Rev. Charles B. Ray: 'Needless Difficulties to be Encountered by an Agent'
articles.author: C. B. Ray, General Agent
articles.summary: General Agent Charles B. Ray writes on the obstacles a traveling subscription agent faces -- economic hardship as a pretext, abolitionist readers who feel already over-subscribed to other papers, and above all indecisive ministers and church trustees who stall on scheduling a public meeting for his visit -- and appeals to Black clergy and congregations nationwide to organize promptly when an agent arrives, since he can visit each place only once a year.

articles.headline: Dixon fugitive-slave case -- Supreme Court motion to quash the writ De Homine Replegiando
articles.summary: Reprints the formal notice of motion (Albany, July 1837) by Walter T. Allender's attorney John A. Morrell to quash the writ De Homine Replegiando obtained on behalf of William Dixon by attorney H. Dresser, plus the court's order suspending decision until the next term and directing the constitutionality question be referred toward the Court for the Correction of Errors -- continuing the Dixon fugitive-slave case covered in prior issues.

articles.headline: Samuel E. Cornish denied service at Pattinson's Eating House
articles.author: Samuel E. Cornish
articles.summary: Editor Samuel E. Cornish recounts being refused a cup of tea at Pattinson's eating house in New York (August 10, 1837) on account of his color -- the first such refusal in his experience there despite years of patronizing the city's public establishments and traveling as far as Niagara Falls without incident. Notes the proprietor's excuse was fear of losing other customers, and signs the piece as editor of the Colored American.

articles.headline: Constitution of the Moral Reform Society of Cranberry (NJ)
articles.summary: Full constitution of the newly formed Moral Reform Society of Cranberry, NJ, an auxiliary to the American Moral Reform Society, dedicated to educating and morally uplifting the local Black population; approximately fifty members at founding. First officers: Francis Styke, President; Edward Ditmus, 1st Vice President; Ceasar Higgins, 2nd Vice President; Richard Valentine, Corresponding Secretary; Lewis F. Ditmus, Recording Secretary; George Trindall, Treasurer; managers Joseph Hawkins, John Douglass, Aaron B. Beard, Peter Merrile, John Napus, Joseph Handley, Hezekiah Loid, and Richard Onque.

events.name: Third Anniversary of British West India Emancipation -- Broadway Tabernacle, New York
events.description: The United Anti-Slavery Society of New York's third-anniversary celebration of British West India Emancipation drew roughly 3,500 attendees. Petitions to Congress and the New York Legislature for abolition of slavery in the District of Columbia, abolition in New York, and against Texas annexation were circulated and signed throughout the day. The evening meeting was chaired by Lewis Tappan, with addresses by James A. Thome (recently returned from a fact-finding visit to the British West Indies), Rev. Theodore S. Wright, and Rev. Nathaniel Colver; a commemorative hymn by J. G. Whittier was sung.

events.name: Public meeting to organize New York suffrage petitions -- Phoenix Hall
events.location: Phoenix Hall, Anthony St., New York City
events.description: Public meeting called for Wednesday, August 9, 1837 at Phoenix Hall to organize petitions to the New York State Legislature for the right of suffrage, and to authorize traveling agents of the Colored American to circulate the petitions and collect signatures statewide.

events.name: Important Meeting of Colored Citizens of New York -- Phoenix Room
events.description: Public meeting of the colored citizens of New York held at the Phoenix Room resolving to petition the state legislature at its next session for the right of suffrage, and appointing a committee to draft credentials for the paper's proprietor (P. A. Bell) and agent (C. B. Ray) as they traveled the state to organize the petition drive.
